questions.question: What happens when an FCM message arrives and the app is in the background?
options: FCM automatically builds and shows the notification
questions.explanation: When the app is in the background or killed, FCM automatically displays the notification if the message contains a 'notification' payload. For data-only payloads, onMessageReceived() fires even in the background.

questions.question: Starting from Android 8 (API 26), what is required to show notifications?
questions.explanation: Android 8+ requires notifications to be assigned to a NotificationChannel. Without one, notifications will silently fail. Channels let users control notification behavior per category.

questions.question: On Android 13+, what new requirement was added for notifications?
options: You must request the POST_NOTIFICATIONS runtime permission
All notifications must use a foreground service
questions.explanation: Android 13 (API 33) introduced the POST_NOTIFICATIONS runtime permission. Without requesting and being granted this permission, your notifications won't be shown.

questions.question: Which FCM payload type gives you full control over notification display in both foreground and background?
questions.explanation: Data-only payloads trigger onMessageReceived() in both foreground AND background, giving you full control to build custom notifications. The 'notification' payload is auto-handled in background.

questions.question: Why does a notification sometimes not pop up as a heads-up display?
options: The app is not installed correctly
The NotificationChannel importance is too low
Heads-up is not supported on Android
questions.explanation: Heads-up notifications require IMPORTANCE_HIGH on the NotificationChannel. If the channel is set to DEFAULT, LOW, or MIN, the notification will appear silently in the status bar.

questions.question: What does the onNewToken() callback in FirebaseMessagingService do?
options: It refreshes the user's auth token
It fires when a new FCM device token is generated, so you can sync it with your server
It creates a new notification channel
questions.explanation: onNewToken() is called when FCM generates a new device registration token (first run or token refresh). You must send this token to your server to target this device for push notifications.

questions.question: Which PendingIntent flag is recommended for most notification click actions?
questions.explanation: FLAG_IMMUTABLE is recommended for most notification PendingIntents for security. Use FLAG_MUTABLE only when you specifically need to modify the PendingIntent later (e.g., inline reply).

questions.question: What happens to the FCM token after a factory reset?
options: A new token is generated and onNewToken() fires
questions.explanation: After a factory reset, a completely new FCM token is generated. onNewToken() fires and you must sync the new token with your server. The old token becomes invalid.

questions.question: How can you send a notification to multiple devices subscribed to a topic?
options: Send individual messages to each device token
Use FirebaseMessaging.subscribeToTopic() and target the topic
questions.explanation: Devices subscribe to topics using FirebaseMessaging.getInstance().subscribeToTopic(). Your server then targets '/topics/topic_name' to send notifications to all subscribed devices at once.

questions.question: Why might clicking a notification sometimes open the wrong screen?
options: PendingIntent requestCode was reused with FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT
questions.explanation: When multiple notifications share the same PendingIntent requestCode with FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT, they overwrite each other's intent data. Use unique requestCodes for different notification actions.
